text.en: Who led Jesus into the wilderness?
The Spirit. "Jesus was led by the Spirit into the wilderness to be tempted by the devil."
What did the devil ask Jesus to do first: turn stones into bread, jump from the temple, or worship him for the kingdoms of the world?
Turn stones into bread — the devil says, "If You are the Son of God, tell these stones to become bread."
This passage names three places: the wilderness, where the Spirit led Jesus (v.1), and two more places the devil took him afterward. Name all three, in the order they happen.

In order: the wilderness, where the Spirit led Jesus and the devil first tempted him (v.1-3); the holy city, on the pinnacle of the temple (v.5); and a very high mountain, showing him all the kingdoms of the world (v.8).

Each time Jesus answers the devil, he appeals to Scripture: "It is written" (v.4), "It is also written" (v.7), "For it is written" (v.10). What do all three openings point to every time before Jesus gives his answer?

Scripture — what God has already spoken in his written Word. Before each answer Jesus takes his stand on what "is written" rather than on a new word invented for the moment. He does so as the Son who honors Scripture as God's own settled truth; the point is where he grounds his reply (the written Word), not that he is without authority of his own.
Jesus ends with "serve Him only" (v.10), right after the devil's offer in verse 9. What work does the little word "only" do here — what does it include, and what does it rule out?
"Only" makes worship and service exclusive: God alone is to be worshiped and served, and this rules out the devil's own offer in v.9 — falling down to worship him for the kingdoms of the world.
Without looking back at the passage, try to say back one of the lines Jesus quoted from Scripture when the devil tempted him (for example, his answer about bread in verse 4).

Any one of the three lines, recited close to word: (v.4) "It is written: Man shall not live on bread alone, but on every word that comes from the mouth of God." (v.7) "It is also written: Do not put the Lord your God to the test." (v.10) "For it is written: Worship the Lord your God and serve Him only." Credit any answer that is substantially one of these three, not a paraphrase invented from scratch.

Jesus's answer in verse 4, "Man shall not live on bread alone, but on every word that comes from the mouth of God," is a quotation from Deuteronomy 8:3. Read that cross-reference: what was Israel's own experience in the wilderness that this line was first spoken about?

In the wilderness God let Israel hunger and then fed them with manna, to teach them that man does not live by bread alone but by every word that comes from the mouth of the LORD. Jesus, hungry after forty days, answers the devil with the same lesson Israel was given.

In verse 6 the devil quotes a promise about angels protecting the Son of God — this is Psalm 91:11-12. Jesus replies in verse 7 with "Do not put the Lord your God to the test," a quotation from Deuteronomy 6:16. Read both cross-references: how does Jesus use one Scripture (a command) to answer the devil's misuse of another (a promise)?

The devil quotes a promise (Ps 91:11-12) to justify a reckless act; Jesus answers not by disputing the promise but by citing a command (Deu 6:16, "do not put the Lord your God to the test") that governs how any promise may rightly be claimed. Scripture interprets Scripture — a promise is never a license to presume on God.
After reading this passage, what is a question you have about the wilderness, or about the devil, or about the angels at the end?

Fully open — there is no expected answer. Children may wonder about the wilderness, who the devil is, why the Spirit led Jesus into temptation, or why the angels only appear at the end. Receive whatever question they bring rather than steering them toward one you'd prefer; if pressed, it's fine to say some of it stays a mystery.
What do you wonder about why the devil waited until Jesus had fasted forty days before tempting him with bread?

The passage itself notes the timing: forty days of fasting, then hunger (v.2), then the tempter comes (v.3) — the trial strikes at the point of greatest physical need. Let the child sit with that connection rather than supplying a settled explanation of the devil's motive or timing, which the text does not spell out.
Jesus answers all three temptations by quoting Scripture. Based on what the passage shows him doing, why do you think he chose to answer this way?

The text shows Jesus meeting all three temptations by taking his stand on what is written — grounding each answer in God's written Word rather than in a new word of his own, though as the Son he is himself its author (see the brief: he honors Scripture as an example to us, and answers one Scripture's misuse with another). Let the leader draw out reasons the youth offers — trust in God's word, resisting the devil, following God's own pattern — without collapsing it to a single correct phrase.
The devil offers Jesus "all the kingdoms of the world and their glory" in exchange for worship (vv.8-9). What does Jesus's refusal in verse 10 say about what he considers to belong to God alone?

Jesus refuses power and glory offered on the condition of worshiping someone other than God, and answers with "Worship the Lord your God and serve Him only" (v.10) — worship and ultimate allegiance belong to God alone. Let the discussion stay open to what this costs or means in the youth's own life rather than closing it with a formula.

Jesus met temptation by speaking God's Word out loud. Which line from this passage (v.4, v.7, or v.10) will you keep ready for the next time you are tempted — and where could you write it down so you will see it?

The point is personal readiness, not a right answer — any of the three quoted lines (v.4, v.7, v.10) is a legitimate choice depending on what temptation the person names for themselves. Let them choose and name their own place to write it down rather than assigning one.

Fold a paper into three panels. In each panel draw one of the three places from this passage: the wilderness (where the Spirit led Jesus), the temple, and the mountain (where the devil took him). Under each panel, write just one or two words for what happened there.

Panel 1 — the wilderness (v.1-3): fasting, hunger, devil tempts with bread. Panel 2 — the holy city / pinnacle of the temple (v.5-7): devil tempts him to throw himself down. Panel 3 — a very high mountain (v.8-10): devil offers the kingdoms of the world for worship. Use this to check each panel is in the right place and order.
Draw Jesus in the wilderness. Draw the devil next to him. At the end of your drawing, add angels, because the passage says angels came to Jesus after the devil left.
Listen for what happens right after Jesus finishes fasting forty days and forty nights.
He was hungry — and the tempter came to him and said, "If You are the Son of God, tell these stones to become bread."
Listen for the short words Jesus uses each time to point to Scripture before he answers the devil.

category.en: Listen for how Jesus introduces Scripture

text.en: "It is written" (v.4), "It is also written" (v.7), "For it is written" (v.10) — each introduces a quotation of Scripture just before Jesus answers.
Listen for what the devil offers Jesus in the third temptation, and for the reason Jesus gives when he refuses.

category.en: Listen for an offer and a refusal

text.en: The devil offers "all the kingdoms of the world and their glory" in exchange for Jesus falling down to worship him (vv.8-9); Jesus refuses, citing worship and service that belong to God alone (v.10). Let adults connect this to their own places of trading allegiance for gain, without prescribing the connection for them.
But Jesus answered, “It is written: ‘Man shall not live on bread alone, but on every word that comes from the mouth of God.’” (Matthew 4:4)
In your own words, retell what happened when Jesus was tempted in the wilderness: where he was, what the devil asked him to do each of the three times, how Jesus answered, and how the encounter ends.

Checklist for a complete retelling: Jesus is led by the Spirit into the wilderness and fasts forty days (v.1-2); the devil tempts him three times — to turn stones to bread (v.3-4), to throw himself from the temple (v.5-7), and to worship the devil for the kingdoms of the world (v.8-10); each time Jesus answers by quoting Scripture ("it is written"); the devil leaves, and angels come and minister to Jesus (v.11).
Who came to Jesus after he sat down on the mountain?
His disciples came to him. (The crowds are present and within hearing, but the text names the disciples specifically as the ones who 'came to him.')
Where did Jesus go before he began to teach?
Up on the mountain -- he went up on the mountain (and sat down there) before teaching.
Put these in the order they happen: Jesus opens his mouth to teach; Jesus sees the crowds; the disciples come to him; Jesus sits down.

Correct order: 1) Jesus sees the crowds; 2) Jesus sits down (on the mountain, where he had gone up); 3) the disciples come to him; 4) Jesus opens his mouth to teach. 'Sat down' marks a formal, solemn discourse -- Jesus was positioning himself to be heard, not making a passing remark.
As we read, listen for who comes close to Jesus once he sits down on the mountain.
Listen for: his disciples -- they are the ones who come to him once he is seated on the mountain.

These four moments are printed out of order: 'sits down,' 'disciples come to him,' 'sees the crowds,' 'goes up the mountain.' Write the number 1 to 4 next to each to show the order they really happen in.
1) 'sees the crowds'; 2) 'goes up the mountain'; 3) 'sits down'; 4) 'disciples come to him.' Use this to check children's numbering.
Draw Jesus sitting on the mountain, with his disciples coming to him.
In your own words, retell what happens right before Jesus begins to teach: what he sees, where he goes, and who comes to him.

A complete retelling should touch: Jesus sees the crowds; he goes up on the mountain and sits down (a deliberate, formal posture for teaching); his disciples come to him; he then opens his mouth and begins to teach. Listen for these beats, not exact wording.
Picture Jesus going up the mountain, sitting down, and his disciples coming to him just before he begins to teach. What question would you want to ask about this moment?

This is a wonder question with no fixed answer -- let responses range freely. The text itself offers only spare detail (seeing, ascending, sitting, being approached, speaking), so resist supplying invented specifics (what Jesus was thinking, the disciples' emotions, the crowd's reaction) as if the passage stated them. If helpful, you may note the deliberateness of the scene -- Jesus sees, goes up, sits, is approached, then speaks -- as a prompt for noticing, not as the 'right' question to land on.
Without looking, name at least three of the kinds of people Jesus calls "blessed" in this passage (for example, "those who mourn" or "the merciful").

Any three of: the poor in spirit (theirs is the kingdom of heaven), those who mourn (they will be comforted), the meek (they will inherit the earth), those who hunger and thirst for righteousness (they will be filled), the merciful (they will be shown mercy), the pure in heart (they will see God), the peacemakers (they will be called sons of God), those persecuted for righteousness (theirs is the kingdom of heaven).
Reading verses 3-12, which "Blessed are..." saying does Jesus give first, and which "Blessed are..." saying is the last one?

First: "Blessed are the poor in spirit, for theirs is the kingdom of heaven" (v.3). Last "Blessed are..." saying: "Blessed are you when people insult you, persecute you, and falsely say all kinds of evil against you because of Me" (v.11).
Through verse 10, Jesus speaks about 'they' and 'those.' In verses 11-12, who is He speaking to instead, and what word marks the change?
Through v.10 Jesus speaks about others in the third person ("they"/"those"); in vv.11-12 He turns to direct address. The word "you" in "Blessed are you" marks the change.

Jesus pairs each kind of person with a promise. For "poor in spirit" (v.3), "meek" (v.5), and "pure in heart" (v.8), what promise does Jesus attach to each — and what does that promise suggest the phrase means?

Poor in spirit (v.3) -> "theirs is the kingdom of heaven," suggesting humble, needy dependence on God rather than material poverty. Meek (v.5) -> "they will inherit the earth," suggesting quiet, gentle submission is rewarded, not weakness or being overlooked. Pure in heart (v.8) -> "they will see God," suggesting inward, not merely ceremonial, holiness that gives access to God.
"Blessed are the peacemakers, for they will be called sons of God." — Matthew 5:9
Psalm 37:11 also says the meek will inherit the land. How does that verse match what Jesus promises the meek in Matthew 5:5?

Psalm 37:11 already promises "the meek shall inherit the land" — Jesus repeats this same promise almost word for word, showing the blessing on the meek is not a new idea but Scripture's own settled promise.
Psalm 34:18 says the LORD is near the brokenhearted and saves those crushed in spirit. How does that psalm connect to Jesus' first blessing on the 'poor in spirit' in Matthew 5:3?

Psalm 34:18 says the LORD is near the brokenhearted and saves those crushed in spirit — the same humble, needy, self-distrusting heart Jesus calls "poor in spirit" and pronounces blessed with the kingdom of heaven.
Revelation 21:4 says God will wipe away every tear and there will be no more mourning. How does that promise connect to Jesus' words to those who mourn in Matthew 5:4?
Revelation 21:4 describes the final, complete fulfillment of Jesus' promise that those who mourn "will be comforted" — God Himself wiping away every tear so that mourning is no more.
Which one of these eight blessings do you wonder about most? Write down a question you have about it.

Open-ended by design — there is no expected answer to supply. If someone is unsure where to start, you may point back to any one of the eight "Blessed are..." sayings (vv.3-10) or the turn to "you" in vv.11-12, but let their own question stand rather than resolving it for them.

The people Jesus calls 'blessed' — those who mourn, the meek, the persecuted — are not usually the people the world calls fortunate. From what the passage itself promises each of them, why does Jesus call them blessed anyway?

The text's own answer lies in what each "Blessed are..." clause promises (kingdom, comfort, inheritance, fullness, mercy, seeing God, sonship, kingdom again) rather than in circumstances the world would call fortunate. Let the group work from those promises rather than supplying a summary verdict; avoid steering toward either "the suffering itself is the good thing" or "these traits earn the reward" — the text pronounces blessing, it does not explain away the hardship.

Verses 3 and 10 both say "theirs is the kingdom of heaven." Look at how Jesus states it — "Blessed are... for theirs is...": does the wording present the kingdom as something these people earn by their qualities, or as the promise He attaches to them? Point to the words that guide your answer.

Notice the grammar: "Blessed are... for theirs is..." attaches the kingdom as a promise that follows the blessing, not a wage earned by the quality named. Common misreading to watch for: treating "poor in spirit" as a merit that purchases the kingdom. Let the group reason from the wording itself rather than announcing the promise-versus-merit distinction as a settled rule.
This week, do one specific act of peacemaking — help settle a disagreement, or say a kind word to someone you've been at odds with. Write down what you did and what happened.

No right answer to check for. Look for whether a genuine, specific act took place (not a vague intention) and listen to what happened. Record what was done as evidence — do not judge whether the peacemaking "succeeded."
Choose one act of mercy to do this week — something kind for someone who needs help. Write or draw what you did.

No right answer to check for. Look for a specific, concrete act of mercy and what happened, not for a particular kind of deed. Record what was done as evidence, without judging its size or effectiveness.
Listen as the verses are read aloud. Every time you hear the word 'blessed,' hold up one finger. Count how many fingers you have at the end!
The word "blessed" is said nine times: once at the start of each of the eight sayings in vv.3-10, and once more at the start of v.11 ("Blessed are you").
Listen for how many separate "Blessed are..." sayings about other people Jesus gives before He turns and says "Blessed are you" in verse 11.
Eight separate "Blessed are..." sayings about other people (vv.3-10) come before Jesus turns and says "Blessed are you" in v.11.
Listen for the phrase 'kingdom of heaven' — notice how many times it's said and which blessings it's attached to.
"Kingdom of heaven" is said twice: attached to the poor in spirit (v.3) and to those persecuted for righteousness (v.10) — the phrase brackets the whole set of sayings, opening and closing it.
Listen for where Jesus shifts from 'they' to 'you' in verses 11-12, and notice why He links His disciples' reproach to the prophets before them.

Have the group notice that vv.11-12 move from describing others ("they") to addressing the listener directly ("you"), and that Jesus ties the disciples' coming reproach to how "the prophets before you" were treated — placing them in a long line of faithful suffering rather than an isolated hardship. Let listeners voice what that link suggests rather than stating the conclusion for them.
In your own words, retell the blessings Jesus gives in this passage, in order, and what each one promises.

In order: poor in spirit -> kingdom of heaven; those who mourn -> comforted; the meek -> inherit the earth; those who hunger and thirst for righteousness -> filled; the merciful -> shown mercy; the pure in heart -> see God; the peacemakers -> called sons of God; those persecuted for righteousness -> kingdom of heaven; and "you" when insulted, persecuted, and falsely accused for Christ's sake -> a great reward in heaven, like the prophets before you.

Jesus calls his followers "the salt of the earth," and warns that salt which "loses its savor" becomes useless and cannot be made salty again. Since salt is only useful while it stays salty, what might Jesus be saying his followers must keep, to be useful as his "salt"?

Their saltiness itself -- the distinctive quality that makes them useful as salt. Jesus's warning is that salt is only 'salt' while it keeps that savor; if it loses it, it is fit only to be discarded. So disciples must keep whatever it is that makes them 'salt' -- their distinctive, preserving influence -- rather than let it go flat.
Trace, in order, what verse 13 says happens to salt that has lost its taste (what it is good for, then what is done with it).
In order: it is 'no longer good for anything' -- then it is 'thrown out and trampled by men.'

Jesus says, "You are the light of the world. A city on a hill cannot be hidden." What does the picture of a city on a hill show about light — is it meant to be seen or hidden? How does the lamp in verse 15 make the same point?

Light is meant to be seen, not hidden -- a city on a hill cannot be hidden; it's inescapably visible. The lamp in v.15 makes the same point: no one lights a lamp to hide it under a basket; it is set on a stand precisely so it can give light.

Put these in the order the passage gives them: (a) the salt statement, (b) the light and lamp statements, (c) the purpose — letting your light shine so others see your good deeds and glorify the Father. Which one does Jesus place last?

Order: (a) the salt statement (v.13), then (b) the light and lamp statements (vv.14-15), then (c) the purpose (v.16). Jesus places the purpose -- that others see good deeds and glorify the Father -- last.

Verse 15 says people do not light a lamp and put it under a basket, but set it on a stand instead. According to the passage, what does putting it on the stand accomplish that the basket would prevent?

The text states it plainly: on a stand, the lamp 'gives light to everyone in the house' -- under a basket, that light would be blocked from those it's meant to reach. Help the child find that phrase in v.15. Where you can leave it open is what this means for a disciple's life: don't hand them the application (e.g., 'so be seen'); let them sit with why Jesus assumes no one would deliberately hide a lit lamp.

Verse 16 says 'let your light shine before men, that they may see your good deeds and glorify your Father in heaven.' According to this verse, who is meant to receive the glory when good deeds are seen? Why does that matter for how a good deed should be done?

V.16 names the Father in heaven as the one meant to receive glory when good deeds are seen -- not the disciple who does them; that part is answerable straight from the text. Leave the second half open: let the family reason toward motive (done to point to God, not to draw admiration to oneself) rather than supplying that conclusion. Watch for the common misreading that visibility itself is the goal -- the verse's point is who gets the glory, not simply being seen.
Name one specific, doable thing you could do this week that others could see — a good deed like those in verse 16 — done not so people admire you, but so that those who see it would praise God.

Keep this open -- any concrete, observable act is fair game; the passage doesn't specify which deeds. Hold the line on motive from v.16: the deed is done 'that they may... glorify your Father,' not so the doer is admired. If an answer is framed around being noticed or praised personally, gently redirect toward whose glory the act should point to, without prescribing which act to choose.

On an index card, write one observable good deed you will do before the next family study (a specific chore, kind word, or help offered). Note who might see it — and, beside it, write that it is meant to point them to God, not to you (verse 16). Afterward, write what happened.

This extends the same aim onto paper: any specific, observable deed is acceptable content for the card. The leader's role is only to confirm the two pieces v.16 asks for are present -- something observable, and a stated aim that it points to God rather than to the child -- not to approve or steer toward a particular deed. Leave 'what happened' open too; it's a record of what occurred, not a grade.
"In the same way, let your light shine before men, that they may see your good deeds and glorify your Father in heaven." (Matthew 5:16)
Finish the line from memory: 'Let your ___ shine before men, that they may see your ___ and glorify your Father in heaven.'
'Let your light shine before men, that they may see your good deeds and glorify your Father in heaven.'

1 Peter 2:12 tells believers to keep their conduct honorable among outsiders so that, seeing their good deeds, they glorify God. How does this connect to what Matthew 5:16 says should happen when others see your good deeds?

Both point to the same pattern: visible conduct leads observers to glorify God. Matthew 5:16 says to let your light shine so that people, seeing your good deeds, glorify the Father in heaven; 1 Peter 2:12 gives the same instruction -- keep conduct honorable among outsiders so that, seeing your good deeds, they glorify God. Peter's charge is essentially an application of what Jesus commands in v.16.
Philippians 2:15 tells believers to 'shine as lights' in the world. How does this compare with Jesus calling his hearers 'the light of the world' in Matthew 5:14?

Both describe believers as light in a dark setting. Matthew 5:14 calls Jesus's hearers 'the light of the world'; Philippians 2:15 tells believers to 'shine as lights' amid a crooked generation. Philippians makes explicit what Matthew states as fact -- disciples, identified as light, are to actively shine amid surrounding darkness.
What is one question you have about being 'salt' or 'light' after hearing this passage? Write it down to ask during family time.

Fully open -- there is no expected question here. Receive whatever the child raises without correcting or resolving it on the spot; the point is that they notice a real gap or wonder in their own understanding and hold onto it for family discussion, not that the leader supplies the missing answer now.

Draw two pictures: one of a lamp hidden under a basket (dark), and one of a lamp set up on a stand (bright, lighting the room). Ask a grown-up to read v.15 again and point to which picture matches what Jesus says people actually do.

The picture that matches v.15 is the lamp set on the stand, bright and lighting the room -- that's what the passage says people actually do. The hidden-under-a-basket picture is the example Jesus says does not happen ('neither do people... put it under a basket').

category.en: Listen for the names Jesus gives

text.en: As this is read aloud, listen for the two things Jesus tells his listeners they already ARE. Hold up a finger for each one you hear.
Two fingers: 'the salt of the earth' (v.13) and 'the light of the world' (v.14).
In your own words, retell Matthew 5:13-16: what Jesus says about salt, what he says about light, and what he says the point of it all is.

A complete retelling covers three parts, in order: salt -- you are salt, but salt that loses its savor is useless and gets thrown out (v.13); light -- you are light, and light (a city on a hill, a lamp) is not hidden but set out to be seen (vv.14-15); the point -- let that light/good deeds be seen so that people glorify the Father in heaven, not the disciple (v.16). Use it to check the retelling hits all three, not to require exact wording.
